Skip to the content.

← Back to Home

Price Transparency
in Healthcare

Analyzing 6K rows by filtering out from 7.2 million raw hospital pricing records across three Louisville, Kentucky hospitals to expose negotiation gaps, payer disparities, and contract anomalies.

Python pandas Power BI DAX Star Schema CMS Price Transparency
Background

Why Hospital Pricing Data Is Hard to Use

Since 2021, the CMS Hospital Price Transparency Rule requires every U.S. hospital to publish machine-readable files disclosing their gross charges, cash prices, and payer-negotiated rates. In practice, these files arrive in incompatible formats — some wide with hundreds of payer columns, others long with one row per payer — making cross-hospital comparison nearly impossible without significant data engineering.

This project built a full pipeline: automated format detection, layout-aware parsing, normalization into a star schema, and a Power BI dashboard — transforming 7.2 million raw records into 6,681 analysis-ready rows covering 48 high-impact procedures.

CPT Procedures 48 Inpatient, outpatient, lab, pharmacy
Unique Payers 46 Across 53 distinct plan types
Analysis Rows 6,681 From 7.2M raw records
Max Price Spread $122K Same procedure, different payer
Cash vs Min Rate $1.8K Avg premium paid uninsured
Data Sources

Three Hospitals, Three File Formats

Data was collected from publicly available CMS Hospital Price Transparency files. Each hospital publishes a different file structure — a real-world data engineering challenge that required format auto-detection before any analysis could begin.

University of Louisville Health
Louisville, KY
379 Columns
4,423 Output rows
40 Payer contracts
Baptist Health
Louisville, KY
28 Columns
420 Output rows
28 Payer contracts
Norton Hospital
Louisville, KY
30 Columns
1,838 Output rows
13 Payer contracts

The engineering challenge: UofL's file used a wide format with one column per payer (379 total), requiring a melt operation to convert 97 rows into 1,940 long-format rows. Norton and Baptist used long format but had ragged meta rows — the hospital name rows contained fewer columns than the data rows, causing pandas' default C parser to crash. The fix required a two-pass read strategy: meta rows and data rows loaded separately, then combined.

Analysis Goals

Business Questions This Dashboard Answers

  • Q1 Which hospital charges the highest gross charge for the same CPT code — and how does their negotiated rate compare to competitors?
  • Q2 For which procedures does the negotiated rate exceed the gross charge — and which payers and hospitals are involved?
  • Q3 Which payer consistently negotiates the lowest rates, and which plan type (Medicare Advantage, Commercial, Managed Medicaid) gets the deepest discount?
  • Q4 How does the cash price compare to the minimum negotiated rate — are uninsured patients paying more than the best-insured patients?
  • Q5 Which procedures show the widest min-to-max negotiated rate spread, indicating the most volatile or inconsistent payer contracting?
  • Q6 How complete is each hospital's gross charge disclosure — and does incomplete disclosure correlate with higher negotiated rates?
Data Model

Star Schema Design

The cleaned data was modeled as a star schema with one fact table and three dimension tables. Key design decisions were driven by the data itself — min_negotiated and max_negotiated belong in the fact table (not DimProcedure) because Norton stores different ranges per payer row. DimProcedure is keyed on hospital + cpt_code because the same CPT code has different procedure names at each hospital.

Analysis

Key Findings

The scatter plot of gross charge vs. negotiated rate — with a 45° reference line where rate = gross charge — is the analytical centerpiece. Dots below the line indicate successful negotiation. Dots above the line are contract anomalies. What the data revealed was more varied than expected.

Contract Anomaly
CPT 871 — Negotiated Rate Exceeds Gross Charge
At Baptist Health, CPT 871 shows a gross charge of $61,621 with a negotiated rate of $23,961 — sitting visibly above the diagonal reference line. This means the contracted payer rate exceeds the hospital's own listed price, representing a direct financial loss and a contract management failure that warrants immediate audit.
$61,621 → $23,961 above line
Best Negotiation
CPT 470 — 74% Discount on Joint Replacement
CPT 470 (Major Joint Replacement — hip/knee) at Baptist Health shows the deepest payer discount in the dataset. With a gross charge of $122,570 and an average negotiated rate of $31,844, payers achieved approximately a 74% reduction from list price — the furthest dot below the diagonal. High procedure volume gives payers strong leverage on joint replacements.
74% below gross charge
Data Gap
18% Gross Charge Non-Disclosure at Norton & UofL
Baptist Health disclosed gross charges for 100% of procedure rows. Norton Hospital disclosed 83.24% and UofL Health 82.16% — leaving approximately 1 in 6 rows without a gross charge. Without this figure, patients and researchers cannot calculate the true payer discount, undermining the purpose of the CMS transparency mandate.
Baptist 100% · Norton 83% · UofL 82%
Market Insight
Norton Holds Firm — Minimal Payer Discounts
Norton Hospital's procedures (CPT codes 291, 292, 193, 310, 603, 872) cluster tightly along the diagonal reference line across the $10K–$40K gross charge range. Payers are achieving minimal discounts off Norton's list price — a pattern consistent with a hospital that holds strong regional market power in Louisville, forcing payers to accept near-list rates.
Rates hug the diagonal across all payers
Patient Impact
Cash Patients Pay $1,800 More Than Best Payer Rate
On average, the minimum payer-negotiated rate across all procedures is $1,800 lower than the cash price charged to uninsured patients. For the highest-cost procedures, this gap widens dramatically — uninsured patients face not the gross charge sticker price, but a cash price that still substantially exceeds what even the most expensive insurer pays.
$1,800 avg premium for uninsured
Outlier Effect
CPT 470 Skews All Hospital-Level Averages
Baptist Health's average negotiated rate of $15K is nearly 8× UofL's $1K average. However, this comparison is misleading — the hospitals bill for fundamentally different procedure mixes. CPT 470's $31,844 rate alone pulls Baptist's average dramatically upward. The median rate ($1,284 vs mean $7,740 overall) is the more honest metric for typical patient experience.
Mean $7,740 · Median $1,284
Technical Detail

Key DAX Measures

Several measures required careful handling of null gross charge values — Norton and UofL left ~18% of rows without a gross charge, which caused Power BI to suppress those hospitals from any visual using naive aggregations. Each measure below explicitly excludes blank gross charges before dividing.

DAX — Average Negotiated Rate (null-safe)
Avg Negotiated Rate =
CALCULATE(
    AVERAGE('Fact_Prices'[negotiated_rate]),
    FILTER(
        Fact_Prices,
        NOT ISBLANK(Fact_Prices[negotiated_rate]) &&
        Fact_Prices[negotiated_rate] >= 50  -- exclude data-entry noise
    )
)
DAX — Discount % Off Gross (handles missing gross charges)
Discount % Off Gross =
VAR AvgNeg =
    AVERAGE(Fact_Prices[negotiated_rate])
VAR AvgGross =
    CALCULATE(
        AVERAGE(Fact_Prices[gross_charge]),
        NOT ISBLANK(Fact_Prices[gross_charge])
    )
RETURN
    IF(
        NOT ISBLANK(AvgGross),
        1 - DIVIDE(AvgNeg, AvgGross),
        BLANK()
    )
DAX — Overage Amount (calculated column on Fact_Prices)
Overage Amount =
IF(
    Fact_Prices[rate_exceeds_gross] = "Yes",
    Fact_Prices[negotiated_rate] - Fact_Prices[gross_charge],
    BLANK()
)
-- Use as a calculated COLUMN (not measure) to display
-- row-by-row in the pricing anomalies table visual
DAX — Gross Charge Coverage % by Hospital
Gross Charge Coverage =
DIVIDE(
    CALCULATE(
        COUNTROWS(Fact_Prices),
        NOT ISBLANK(Fact_Prices[gross_charge])
    ),
    COUNTROWS(Fact_Prices),
    0
)
-- Baptist = 100% · Norton = 83.24% · UofL = 82.16%
Scope

Procedures Included in Analysis

48 CPT codes were selected spanning emergency, surgical, imaging, laboratory, and inpatient services — chosen to enable meaningful cross-hospital comparison where the same standardized code appears at more than one hospital.

99285 · ED Visit 470 · Knee Replacement 29827 · Shoulder Arthroscopy 45378 · Colonoscopy 45385 · Colonoscopy w/ Polypectomy 43239 · Upper GI Endoscopy 29881 · Knee Meniscectomy 76830 · Pelvic Ultrasound 93306 · Echo w/ Doppler 93451 · Right Heart Cath 72148 · MRI Lumbar 73721 · MRI Knee 73221 · MRI Shoulder 74177 · CT Abdomen & Pelvis 74160 · CT Abdomen 72193 · CT Pelvis 871 · Complex Cardiac Procedure 872 · Cardiac Support 291 · Inpatient Psych 292 · Detox 310 · Medical Rehab 392 · Bone Marrow Transplant 690 · Kidney Procedure 641 · Misc Surgery 603 · Radiation Therapy + 23 additional CPT codes
← Return to Portfolio

Data sourced from CMS Hospital Price Transparency Rule (45 CFR Part 180) · Public domain hospital pricing files
